revision.text:

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|children={{ill|Ahiratsuhime|qid=Q10919837|1=Ahiratsu-hime}}; {{ill|Tensori no Mikoto|qid=Q125364551|1=Tensori no Mikoto}}|father={{ill|Ninigi|qid=Q1056017|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}|mother={{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|qid=Q1781862|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|1=Hoderi}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960|1=Hoori}}|organization={{ill|Yamasachihiko and Umisachihiko|qid=Q11468336|1=Umisachi and Yamasachi}}}}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}}

{{one source|date=October 2021}}
{{Expand Chinese|date=October 2020|topic=cult}}
{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| father = [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
| mother = [[:en:Konohana Sakuya|Konohana Sakuya]]
| children = {{ill|Ahiratsu-hime|en|Ahiratsu-hime|qid=Q10919837|lt=Ahiratsu-hime|1=Ahiratsu-hime}}
| gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}
| parents ={{ill|Ninigi|qid=Q1056017|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|qid=Q1781862|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}| offspring = {{ill|Ahiratsuhime|qid=Q10919837|1=Ahiratsu-hime}}; {{ill|Tensori no Mikoto|qid=Q125364551|1=Tensori no Mikoto}}
| member_of = {{ill|Yamasachihiko and Umisachihiko|qid=Q11468336|1=Umisachi and Yamasachi}}
| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|qid=Q506610|1=Ajisukitakahikone}}; {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|qid=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Hitokotonushi|qid=Q10864079|1=Hitokotonushi}}; {{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|1=Hoderi}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|qid=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}}; {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|qid=Q3090037|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|qid=Q11387080|1=Yamato Okunitama}}; {{ill|honoakari|qid=Q10940411|1=Amenohoakari}}; {{ill|Ōmononushi|qid=Q10936701|1=Ōmononushi}}; {{ill|Ōyamakui|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}
}}{{Nihongo|'''Hosuseri'''|火須勢理命|1=Hosuseri}} is a god that appears in Japanese mythology.<ref>Atsushi Yamakita, "Encyclopedia of Oriental Gods," Shinkigensha , p. 450.</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/ |access-date=2023-03-09 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja |archive-date=2021-10-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/202dd02dd04545/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> Second child of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] and [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> He is considered to be the great-uncle of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (the first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}).

==Overview==

The name Honosusori appears only in the [[Kojiki]] and not in the [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} The description in the "Kojiki" is only that he is the second child of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] and [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], and there is no description of the achievements.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />

According to the [[Kojiki]]: [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] had a brief sexual encounter, after which [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] became pregnant. However, Ninigi doubted whether she could conceive so quickly. Frustrated with Ninigi's suspicion, Sakuyahime built a long birthing hut and set it on fire. Amid the blaze, she gave birth to three male kami<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> - {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi no mikoto|1=Hoderi}} (Umisachi), Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]] (Yamasachi)<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kawai |first=Hayao |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/34574100 |title=Dreams, myths and fairy tales in Japan |last2=河合 |first2=隼雄 |date=1995 |publisher=Daimon |others=隼雄 河合 |isbn=3-85630-544-0 |location=Einsiedeln, Switzerland |pages=85 |oclc=34574100}}</ref> in that order.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> 

== Name meaning ==
The prefix "ho" in their names can mean either "flame" or "heads of grain", while "suseri" is linked to "susumu" (to advance). Hence, their names signify the progression of flames or the steady growth of rice grain.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />
==Genealogy ==
